title: Just in Time (JIT)
summary: 9609 AS — JIT principles, prerequisites, benefits, risks, and when to recommend it.

simpleExplanation.title: Materials arrive exactly when needed
simpleExplanation.summary: JIT delivers components to the production line only as they are required. It frees working capital and cuts storage but needs flawless suppliers and internal discipline.
steps: Parts delivered as required — minimal warehouse stock.
Requires dependable suppliers and quality at source.
Vulnerable to transport strikes, disasters, demand shocks.

flashcards.back: Inventory system where materials arrive just when needed for production.
Reduced holding costs and working capital tied up in stock.
Pioneer of JIT + kaizen; classic case reference.
Reliable suppliers, quality control, flexible workforce, good IT.
Supply disruption stops production immediately — no buffer.
JIT minimises buffer; traditional holds safety stock (4.2.1).
JIT is core to lean production (9.3.4).
Unreliable suppliers, volatile demand, long lead times from abroad.

sections.content: **JIT** (4.2.2) contrasts **4.2.1 buffer stock**. Always **evaluate context** — JIT is not automatically best for a small firm with one unreliable supplier.

sections.question: Car assembler considers JIT for seat deliveries from a supplier 200 km away. Currently holds 3 days' seat stock. Advise whether to adopt JIT.
sections.solution:
**Pros:** Seats are **bulky** — warehouse savings significant; **cash** freed (5.1.2).

**Prerequisites check:**
- Supplier must guarantee **on-time, defect-free** delivery — **quality at source**.
- **IT integration** for production schedule sharing.
- **Backup supplier** or contingency if road blocked.

**Risk:** Single supplier, 200 km — **strike or weather** halts line (costly).

**Recommendation:** **Pilot JIT** on one model line if supplier agrees **penalty clauses**; keep **minimal safety stock** initially — hybrid until proven.
